coredata.dc:title: A sensitive ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on carbon dots and CdTe quantum dots for visual detection of biogenic amines in food samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Jianfeng
Fu, Quanbin
Zhang, Shikai
Liu, Yu
Shi, Xianbao
Hou, Juying
Duan, Junling
Ai, Shiyun
coredata.dc:description: A novel dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensor for biogenic amines (BAs) was prepared by simple mixing blue fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) and yellow fluorescent CdTe quantum dots (CdTe QDs). Based on different sensitive properties of pH, CdTe QDs and CDs were used as the response signal and internal reference signal, respectively. The developed ratiometric fluorescent sensor achieved quantitative analysis of eight kinds of BAs with rapid response (30 s) and low limits of detection (1.259–5.428 μM). Furthermore, color-tunable fluorescent test strips were constructed by easily assembling CDs and CdTe QDs onto filter paper. The obtained smart label showed a distinguishable fluorescent color variation from blue to green during the corruption of shrimp samples. The smart label with advantages of convenience and rapidness provided a method for visually monitoring the freshness of food samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescent sensor
Biogenic amines
Smart label
Visual detection
Food

Visual sensing of BAs in real sample with fluorescent filter papers
Characterization of the N, S-CDs and CdTe QDs
Optical properties of N, S-CDs and CdTe QDs
Effect of pH on fluorescence intensity
The sensitivity of the N, S-CDs@CdTe QDs
Ratiometric fluorescent smart labels for monitoring the freshness of the real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of N, S-CDs (a) and CdTe QDs (b).

HRTEM images of N, S-CDs (a) and CdTe QDs (b) (Inset shows the lattice spacing image of CdTe QDs), particle size distribution of N, S-CDs (c) and CdTe QDs (d), FT-IR spectra of N, S-CDs (e) and CdTe QDs and NAC (f).
UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of N, S-CDs (a) and CdTe QDs (b).
Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs (a), CdTe QDs (b) and N, S-CDs@CdTe QDs (c).

Fluorescence spectra with addition of different concentrations of BAs (left), and linear plots (right) between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F2/F1) and BAs concentration. (a)–(h) were: spermine, spermidine, putrescine, cadaverine, histamine, tryptamine, tyramine, and phenylethylamine, respectively.
Monitoring the freshness of shrimp by using the N,S-CDs@CdTe QDs-FP as smart labels at different time (0–48 h).
Data of N, S-CDs@CdTe QDs for BAs detection.
The comparison of different fluorescent sensors for BAs detection.

head.ce:title: A sensitive ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on carbon dots and CdTe quantum dots for visual detection of biogenic amines in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University, Tai’an, Shandong 271018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University, Tai’an, Shandong 271018, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Shandong Agricultural University, Tai’an, Shandong 271018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Shandong Agricultural University, Tai’an, Shandong 271018, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors contributed equally as first author.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensor for biogenic amines was developed based on carbon dots and CdTe quantum dots. The sensor showed rapid response and low limits of detection. The fluorescent test strips were easily constructed for visually monitoring the freshness of food samples.

A novel dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensor for biogenic amines (BAs) was prepared by simple mixing blue fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) and yellow fluorescent CdTe quantum dots (CdTe QDs). Based on different sensitive properties of pH, CdTe QDs and CDs were used as the response signal and internal reference signal, respectively. The developed ratiometric fluorescent sensor achieved quantitative analysis of eight kinds of BAs with rapid response (30 s) and low limits of detection (1.259–5.428 μM). Furthermore, color-tunable fluorescent test strips were constructed by easily assembling CDs and CdTe QDs onto filter paper. The obtained smart label showed a distinguishable fluorescent color variation from blue to green during the corruption of shrimp samples. The smart label with advantages of convenience and rapidness provided a method for visually monitoring the freshness of food samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Arbitrary disposal during food processing, storage, and consumption would lead to serious public food safety risks. Hence, it is urgent and indispensable to set up a simple, fast and effective food safety monitoring system. Biogenic amines (BAs), as structurally diverse nitrogen-containing organic bases, are produced during metabolism through amination or transamination under the decarboxylation of amino acids by microorganisms, and considered as the biomarker for food freshness and quality [1]. Excessive intake of foods containing BAs (>100 mg/kg in food) may produce toxicological effects such as headache, diarrhea and vomiting, and even posing carcinogenic hazards [2,3]. The European Union restricted histamine content with the maximum limit of 200 mg·kg−1 for fresh fish. The Food and Drug Administration of USA set an admissible limit of 50 mg·kg−1 for histamine in fish products [4]. The maximum residue levels of putrescine and cadaverine in cheeses have been proposed as 180 and 540 mg·kg−1, respectively [5]. Currently, several advanced methods have been established for detecting BAs, including chromatography, Raman spectroscopy, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay, and molecular imprinting [6–9]. Most of these methods suffer from drawbacks in terms of cost-effectiveness and complexity. Optical sensors with advantages of simplicity, fast response and high sensitivity have great potential in the detection of BAs [10].

Luminescence sensing technology has been widely concerned for their advantages in real-time, non-invasive, and visual monitoring of analytes [11]. In particular, smart label monitoring devices loaded with fluorescent sensors have shown great application potential. Based on these advantages, a variety of fluorescence sensors have been developed and applied in the analysis and monitoring of BAs in food [10,12]. However, traditional single-signal luminophore-based fluorescence sensors often fail to achieve the expected effect due to the interference of the instrument background and the external environment (temperature, humidity, light) [13]. In addition, for one luminophore, the typical color transition is almost relatively weak in most cases, leading to errors in visual detection. Recently, the ratiometric fluorescent sensor in the corresponding and assemble mode formed by the layout between the two light-emitting elements achieved higher fidelity resolution and precision. More importantly, the precise ratiometric sensing devices established by rationally tuning different light-emitting elements can achieve strong color transitions visible to the naked-eyes for visual monitoring of analytes [14]. Furthermore, fluorescent test strips prepared by combining sensors with paper-based materials can be employed as portable detection devices, which will achieve effective visual monitoring and cost control.

Semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) and carbon dots (CDs) are considered to be the most widely used luminescent agents in optical sensing due to their modifiable surface groups, great water solubility, and tunable photoluminescence. QDs have excellent photophysical properties, including reliable absorption and emission spectra, high quantum yields, and excellent photochemical stability, thus serving as promising fluorescent sensors [15–17]. Due to the fact that the surface of QDs can be modified with various functional groups and polymers, it is easy to be excited fluorescence resonance energy or electron transfer with the medium, which endows them with a certain pH sensitivity [18]. As a nitrogen-containing organic bases, BAs can be monitored with QDs-based pH sensors. Most of pH-sensitive QDs showed single-wavelength fluorescence, that could be unavoidably affected by background interference and fluctuation of environmental conditions, resulting in a decrease of detection accuracy. Recent studies have indicated that the combination of QDs as dynamic luminophores with a suitable reference luminophore in a ratiometric design can eliminate background interference and fluctuations in detection conditions [19,20]. Therefore, choosing different doped luminescent agents to build an accurate fluorescence sensor will enable accurate BAs determination. CDs, as a new type of green zero-dimension carbon material, have been widely applied in drug delivery, photocatalysis, analytical devices, and bioimaging because of their abundant surface functional groups, great biocompatibility, and simple synthesis [21–24]. Self-calibrating ratiometric fluorescent sensors based on CDs as built-in external references will greatly improve reliability and sensitivity. For BAs monitoring, combination of QDs and CDs as ratiometric fluorescence sensors are rarely reported. In particular, the applications of the CDs@QDs-based test strips for BAs sensing have never been addressed.

Herein, the yellow pH-sensitive CdTe QDs were synthesized via a reflux heating method. Through mixing the blue fluorescent nitrogen and sulfur-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs), a sensitive ratiometric fluorescent sensor (N, S-CDs@CdTe QDs) for BAs sensing was successfully constructed. Upon addition of BAs, the basicity of the medium increased continuously, and the yellow fluorescence of CdTe QDs was gradually excited. While the fluorescence intensity of blue N, S-CDs remained stable. With the establishment of the linear relationship, the quantitative and sensitive analysis of eight kinds of BAs were achieved. Furthermore, a dual-emission fluorescent filter paper (N, S-CDs@CdTe QDs-FP) was constructed for visual detection of BAs via immersing the filter paper into the mixture solution of N, S-CDs@CdTe QDs. The actual sample analysis proved that the N, S-CDs@CdTe QDs-FP could achieve on-site detection of the shrimp freshness. This kind of smart label based on fluorescent test strips realized economical, rapid, intuitive and accurate monitoring of the food freshness, which would provide significant public health implications.

Cadmium chloride (98%), sodium citrate (98%), sodium tellurite (97%), N-Acetyl-L-Cysteine (99%) and L-Cysteine (99%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium borohydride (99%) was provided by BASF Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Citric acid was obtained from Kaitong Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Biogenic amines including cadaverine (98%), spermidine (99%), tryptamine (98%), spermine (98%), tyramine (98%), putrescine (98%), histamine (96%) and β-phenylethylamine (98%) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

The crystal structures of N, S-CDs and CdTe QDs were measured by an X-ray diffractometer (Bruker Optics, Karlsruhe, Germany). The morphologies and sizes of N, S-CDs and CdTe QDs were characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20, FEI, USA). Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra was measured by UV-250 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian, PaloAlto, USA) was used to measure the fluorescence emission spectra with the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Fourier transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded by a Thermo Nicolet 380 spectrophotometer (Bruker Optics, Karlsruhe, Germany) using KBr pellets. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was taken by Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (SWPRATM 55, Carl Zeiss Micro Imaging Co., Ltd., Germany).

Based on the previous literatures with some modification, the N-Acetyl-L-Cysteine (NAC) capped CdTe QDs was synthesized using the reflux method, and the N, S-CDs was prepared via the hydrothermal method [25,26]. The specific synthesis procedures of CdTe QDs and N, S-CDs were shown in supporting information. The CdTe QDs and N, S-CDs were mixed with the volume ratio of 5:3 and the pH of the mixture was adjusted to 5.0 using HCl solution, the N, S-CDs@CdTe QDs was successfully obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of ratiometric filter paper-based smart tag
ce:section.ce:para: The ratiometric filter paper-based smart tag (N, S-CDs@CdTe QDs-FP) was prepared by a dipping method. Briefly, the dual-emission fluorescence sensor solution was prepared by mixing N, S-CDs and CdTe QDs with the volume ratio 5:3. Next, the mixture was treated under ultrasound for 15 min at room temperature. The prepared homogeneous stock solution was adjusted to pH 5.0 using HCl solution. Then, the filter paper was cut into square label with dimensions of 1 cm by 3 cm and placed into the mixture stock solution. Finally, the impregnated filter paper was taken out by tweezers after 30 min and dried in air for further application.

Various BAs were weighed accurately and dissolved with deionized water to obtain the stock solutions at a concentration of 0.1 mol·L−1. Different concentrations of standard solutions were prepared by diluting the stock solution (0.1 mol·L−1) with deionized water and stored at 4 °C in dark. To demonstrate the sensing properties of N, S-CDs@CdTe QDs for BAs, 50 μL N, S-CDs and 30 μL CdTe QDs were added to a series of 5 mL centrifuge tube and the final volume was adjusted to 4 mL using deionized water. Then, the pH of the prepared N, S-CDs@CdTe QDs mixture was adjusted to 5.0 using HCl solution and different concentrations of BAs were added to the sensor solutions. After 30 s, the fluorescence emission spectra of the sensor solutions were measured under the excitation of 360 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual sensing of BAs in real sample with fluorescent filter papers
ce:section.ce:para: Shrimps were purchased from local supermarket and selected as the practical sample. As a protein-based food, large amounts of BAs will be released during the spoilage of shrimps. Shrimps with similar size and freshness were transferred to petri dishes and fluorescent filter papers were attached to the nearest side. The freshness could be recorded with the fluorescent color changes of the smart labels. Under 365 nm UV light irradiation, the fluorescent images of solid filter paper-based smart tag were acquired by a mobile phone to record the color changes at different time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the N, S-CDs and CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: As exhibited in Fig. 1 a, the XRD pattern of N, S-CDs displayed a wide diffraction peak at about 21.4°, which demonstrated the amorphous structure of the N, S-CDs [27]. The XRD pattern of CdTe QDs (Fig. 1b) showed three obvious diffraction peaks at 24.7°, 40.1°, 46.9° with the data records of (111), (220) and (311) crystal planes in the database, respectively [28].

HRTEM images were utilized to characterize the morphologies and sizes of CdTe QDs and N, S-CDs. As displayed in Fig. 2 a, the N, S-CDs exhibited a spherical structure. There was a homogeneously dispersed system in water with no apparent agglomeration phenomenon, which indicated the excellent hydrophilic character of N, S-CDs. 100 individual particles in the HRTEM spectrum were randomly selected, as indicated in Fig. 2c, the particle sizes of N, S-CDs were mainly distributed in the range of 2.0–6.0 nm, with an average diameter of 4.13 nm. As demonstrated in Fig. 2b, the prepared CdTe QDs also exhibited good dispersibility in water. Meanwhile, the inset of Fig. 2b revealed obvious lattice fringes with the spacing of 0.36 nm, which proved the high crystallinity of CdTe QDs [29]. As shown in Fig. 2d, the particle sizes of CdTe QDs were mainly distributed in the range of 2.0–5.0 nm and the average size was 3.38 nm.

Chemical compositions and functional groups of N, S-CDs and CdTe QDs were characterized by FT-IR. The FT-IR spectrum for the N, S-CDs was shown in Fig. 2e, the broad peaks at 3462.12 cm−1 and 3232.25 cm−1 were attributed to OH and NH, respectively [30], while the stretching vibration peaks of CH appeared at 2968.40 cm−1 and 2929.34 cm−1. The strong characteristic absorption peaks at 1704.86 cm−1, 1402.07 cm−1 and 1206.78 cm−1 corresponded to CO, CN, CO stretching vibration [31–33], which proved the presence of carboxylic acid groups.

The stretching vibration of CS and SH bonds appeared at 780.12 cm−1, 633.60 cm−1 and 2609.69 cm−1, which indicated the doping of the S in N, S-CDs [33–35]. The FT-IR spectrum of the CdTe QDs was shown in Fig. 2f, the characteristic stretching vibration at 3373.26 cm−1 proved the existence of OH [36]. Moreover, the peaks at 1575.95 cm−1, 1390.29 cm−1 and 1335.30 cm−1 were ascribed to the existence of COOH [37]. It was worth noting that the disappearance of -SH stretching vibration at 2546.37 cm−1 indicated the successful modification of CdTe QDs with NAC by the covalent affinity reaction between Cd2+ and -SH [36]. These results further suggested that the rich existence of hydrophilic groups in N, S-CDs and CdTe QDs, which might be responsible for high water solubility. XPS spectra was measured to further investigate the element content and functional groups on the surface of N, S-CDs and CdTe QDs. Four characteristic peaks could be observed at 532.08 eV, 400.08 eV, 285.08 eV, 164.08 eV in XPS spectra (Fig. S1a), corresponding to O 1s, N 1s, C 1s and S 2p, respectively. It was worth pointing out that the atomic ratios of N and S were 5.05% and 3.83%, which represented that N and S were successfully doped into carbon dots. Fig. S1(b–e) shows the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, O 1s, N 1s and S 2p, where the functional groups on the surface of N, S-CDs could be observed [26]. Fig. S2 exhibited the XPS spectra and high-resolution XPS spectra of S 2p, Cd 3d, and Te 3d of CdTe QDs, which further illustrated the successful preparation of CdTe QDs [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of N, S-CDs and CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para:
UV–visible absorption and fluorescence emission spectra were carried out to further systematically study the optical properties of N, S-CDs and CdTe QDs. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the N, S-CDs showed a strong absorption peak at 341 nm, that might be related to the n − π* transitions of COOH or NH2 groups [38]. The fluorescence emission spectrum in Fig. 3b exhibited a maximum emission wavelength at about 418 nm with a bright blue emission under the irradiation of 365 nm UV light (inset of Fig. 3a). The absorption peak of the CdTe QDs was at about 546 nm, and the maximum fluorescence emission wavelength could be observed at about 570 nm with a yellow fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet light (inset in Fig. 3b). In addition, UV–vis spectra could be used to estimate the mean particle size of the CdTe QDs based on the following equations:
D =
( 9.8127 ×
10
- 7
)
λ
3
-
( 1.7147 ×
10
- 3
)
λ
2
+
( 1.0064 )
λ - 194.84
where D is the average particle size and λ is the maximum absorption wavelength [28]. The average particle size of CdTe QDs was calculated to be 3.20 nm, which was consistent with the result of HRTEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on fluorescence intensity
ce:section.ce:para: pH-dependent fluorescence spectral response of the N, S-CDs, CdTe QDs and N, S-CDs@CdTe QDs were explored via varying the pH from 1 to 12. HCl and NaOH solution were used to adjust the pH. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the pH (pH > 3) showed slight effect on the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs. In contrast, the CdTe QDs exhibited a sensitive response to pH. As displayed in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs was weak in an acidic environment while it increased gradually with the increase of pH (pH < 10). As shown in Fig. 4c, the N, S-CDs@CdTe QDs showed the similar pH response, the N, S-CDs was pH-insensitive (pH > 3) and the CdTe QDs was pH-sensitive. Since BAs with amine groups are alkaline, adding BAs to the mixture solution of N, S-CDs@CdTe QDs whose pH was initially set as pH 5 would increase the solution pH. As a result, the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs increased with increasing the BAs concentrations while the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs hardly changed. The above results demonstrated that dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensor could be established with the N, S-CDs as the reference signal and CdTe QDs as the response signal for pH sensing (Fig. 4c). Rely on the properties of pH-response, the developed ratiometric fluorescent sensor was a proper choice for the sensitive sensing of BAs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensitivity of the N, S-CDs@CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing performance of the N, S-CDs@CdTe QDs for BAs was studied, obvious changes were observed in their fluorescence spectra after adding various BAs. Eight kinds of BAs were applied to determine the sensitivity of the established method. As shown in Fig. 5 , the fluorescence intensity of the CdTe QDs increased with increasing BAs concentrations, while no obvious change was observed in the fluorescence intensity of the N, S-CDs. The developed method exhibited a significant linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F2/F1) and BAs concentration. The specific data were summarized in Table 1 . For the eight kinds of BAs, the N, S-CDs@CdTe QDs exhibited good linear ranges (0–50 μM, 0–75 μM, and 0–100 μM) with excellent correlation coefficients (R2) (0.9922–0.9987). Furthermore, the low LODs (1.259–5.428 μM) and LOQs (4.198–18.592 μM) indicated the sensing sensitivity of the developed N, S-CDs@CdTe QDs for BAs. The BAs tested in this work have different numbers of nitrogen atom which can accept hydrogen ion. Different BAs show different alkalinity. In comparison, the aliphatic BAs (such as spermine, spermidine, putrescince and cadaverine) contain multiple primary amine groups, they show relatively higher alkalinity. Based on the pH sensitivity of CdTe QDs, the developed N, S-CDs@CdTe QDs showed higher sensitivity for spermine, spermidine, putrescince and cadaverine. As a result, the sensitivities for different BAs were slightly different. Meanwhile, the sensing performance of N, S-CDs@CdTe QDs was compared with those other reported fluorescent materials for BAs sensing. The results in Table 2 showed that the prepared N, S-CDs@CdTe QDs exhibited the advantages of high sensitivity, rapidness and multiple targets.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescent smart labels for monitoring the freshness of the real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Shrimps were used to evaluate the practical application of the smart labels for monitoring the food freshness. Blue fluorescent color represented fresh, and green color represented spoilage. The prepared smart labels and fresh shrimps were fixed in petri dishes which was covered with plastic wrap. A relatively sealed packaging was created and stored at room temperature for 0–48 h. The fluorescent color changes of these smart labels were recorded by a mobile phone under 365 nm UV light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 6 , the N, S-CDs@CdTe QDs-FP showed blue color in the prescence of fresh shrimp. After storage for 12 h at room temperature, the fluorescence color of the N, S-CDs@CdTe QDs-FP changed from blue to green and the fluorescence of the smart label became brighter than before. The green fluorescence of the smart label showed noticeable after storage for 24 h. While the fluorescence color of the N, S-CDs@CdTe-FP became weak gradually after storage for 36 h. That might be related to the pH increase during long time storage. These results revealed that the developed N, S-CDs@CdTe QDs-FP had great prospects in real-time monitoring the freshness of the shrimp with the advantages of portability and visualization.

In summary, a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for BAs based on N, S-CDs and CdTe QDs was developed. Yellow-emitting CdTe QDs were served as indicator signals, and blue-emitting N, S-CDs were used as internal references. The prepared ratiometric fluorescent sensor showed sensitive, fast-responsive, and linear-dependent sensing properties for eight kinds of BAs. Furthermore, the fluorescent filter paper-based sensor with excellent BAs sensing performance was developed by dipping filter paper in N, S-CDs@CdTe QDs solution. With the record of distinguishable fluorescent color variation, the prepared N, S-CDs@CdTe QDs-FP was utilized as a smart label sensor for the real-time monitoring of shrimp freshness. The proposed paper-based sensor achieved fast and visual monitoring of food freshness, which was of great significance for promoting the development of low-cost smart labels for practical applications in food safety. Although the developed N, S-CDs@CdTe QDs achieved high sensitivity and visual monitoring for various BAs, the developed smart label cannot achieve accurate quantitative determination for BAs. We believe that with the continuous advancement of research, this problem will be overcome.

Jianfeng Yan: Writing – original draft. Quanbin Fu: Writing – review & editing. Shikai Zhang: Data curation. Yu Liu: Investigation. Xianbao Shi: Investigation. Juying Hou: Supervision. Junling Duan: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Shiyun Ai: Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 41771342, 21607096), the National Key Research and Development Plan (No. 2017YFD0801504), the Funds of Shandong “Double Tops” Program (No. SYL2017XTTD15) and Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation (ZR2019MD025).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121706.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Amine-responsive cellulose-based ratiometric fluorescent materials for real-time and visual detection of shrimp and crab freshness
Nat. Commun.

Anal. Methods
